pattern: Every local inversion is also a global one, so the counts match exactly when no value sits more than one position away from its sorted index.
hints: Local inversions are a subset of global inversions, so the counts are equal only when there are no extra non-adjacent inversions.
A non-local global inversion appears whenever some value ends up more than one slot from where sorting would place it.
Scan left to right tracking the maximum seen before index i-1; if that running max ever exceeds nums[i] the answer is false, otherwise true.

edge_inputs.description: one adjacent swap keeps the counts equal
a value moved two slots creates an extra global inversion
fully reversed fails for length above two
two-element reversal is still purely local
early large value far from home breaks equality

stress.description: permutations built from the identity with a few adjacent swaps so many cases stay ideal
stress.note: mostly adjacent swaps keep some cases ideal while non-adjacent swaps break others
